Sam Altman just made it clear: Money won't stop OpenAI from building AGI. At Stanford, he said he doesn't care if they burn $50 billion a year - as long as they can pay the bills and create value… | Charlie Gilichibi
Agree & Join LinkedIn
# Charlie Gilichibi’s Post
Charlie Gilichibi
4mo
Sam Altman just made it clear: Money won't stop OpenAI from building AGI. At Stanford, he said he doesn't care if they burn $50 billion a year - as long as they can pay the bills and create value for society. Here's what he actually said and why it matters. The exact quote: At a Stanford eCorner talk, Altman stated: "Whether we burn 500 million a year or 5 billion or 50 billion a year, I don't care. I genuinely don't. As long as we can... stay on a trajectory where [we] create way more value for society... and figure out a way to pay the bills. We're making AGI, it's gonna be expensive. It's totally worth it." This comes from the official Stanford transcript of "The Possibilities of AI." The two conditions that matter: Altman's statement isn't unlimited spending. It has clear conditions: OpenAI can pay the bills (sustainable financing) The outcome creates more value for society than it costs. If both conditions are met, then the actual dollar amount doesn't matter. What this actually means: This isn't Altman announcing "$50 billion budget for next year." This is Altman stating OpenAI's priorities: AGI development comes first, cost containment comes second. If they can finance it and if the result justifies it, spending is essentially uncapped. The key phrase: "figure out a way to pay the bills": OpenAI still needs sustainable financing. This could mean: → Raising capital from investors → Generating revenue from products → Securing strategic partnerships → Some combination of all three Altman isn't saying OpenAI has unlimited money. He's saying they'll find the money if needed. Why this statement is significant: Signal to investors: OpenAI's priority is AGI, not short-term profitability. Be prepared for massive capital needs. Signal to competitors: OpenAI won't lose the AGI race because of budget constraints. They'll outspend whoever they need to. Signal to the industry: AGI development is treated as worth almost any financeable cost. The AGI justification: Altman frames this around societal value, not just commercial success. The implicit argument: AGI is so transformative that nearly any cost is justified if it's developed responsibly and creates sufficient value. This positions OpenAI's mission as bigger than profit maximization. The industry context: Altman's comments align with broader expectations of massive AI infrastructure spending. Industry projections frame total ecosystem spending in the trillions over time - across compute, data centers, energy infrastructure. $50 billion annually for one company would be extraordinary, but not disconnected from where the industry is heading. What this means for the AGI race: If OpenAI is willing to spend whatever they can finance, competitors face a choice: Match that commitment and find similar financing, or explain why lower spending still wins. This is Altman publicly stating: We won't lose on capital deployment. By Anik Singal, 2nd November 2025

If both conditions are met, then the actual dollar amount doesn't matter. What this actually means: This isn't Altman announcing "$50 billion budget for next year." This is Altman stating OpenAI's priorities: AGI development comes first, cost containment comes second. If they can finance it and if the result justifies it, spending is essentially uncapped. The key phrase: "figure out a way to pay the bills": OpenAI still needs sustainable financing. This could mean: → Raising capital from investors → Generating revenue from products → Securing strategic partnerships → Some combination of all three Altman isn't saying OpenAI has unlimited money. He's saying they'll find the money if needed. Why this statement is significant: Signal to investors: OpenAI's priority is AGI, not short-term profitability. Be prepared for massive capital needs. Signal to competitors: OpenAI won't lose the AGI race because of budget constraints. They'll outspend whoever they need to. Signal to the industry: AGI development is treated as worth almost any financeable cost. The AGI justification: Altman frames this around societal value, not just commercial success. The implicit argument: AGI is so transformative that nearly any cost is justified if it's developed responsibly and creates sufficient value. This positions OpenAI's mission as bigger than profit maximization. The industry context: Altman's comments align with broader expectations of massive AI infrastructure spending. Industry projections frame total ecosystem spending in the trillions over time - across compute, data centers, energy infrastructure. $50 billion annually for one company would be extraordinary, but not disconnected from where the industry is heading. What this means for the AGI race: If OpenAI is willing to spend whatever they can finance, competitors face a choice: Match that commitment and find similar financing, or explain why lower spending still wins. This is Altman publicly stating: We won't lose on capital deployment.

The $1 Trillion Trial Balloon 🎈 That Popped in 24 Hours It's been a whirlwind 48 hours for OpenAI, sparking one of the most intense debates yet on the true cost of AGI. If you're just catching up, here’s a quick overview of what happened. 1. The Spark: The "Pre-emptive Bailout" Idea At a Wall Street Journal conference, OpenAI's CFO, Sarah Friar, floated a novel idea for funding the company's colossal infrastructure needs. She suggested that to finance the $1 trillion+ cost of next-gen AI data centers, the company was looking for an "ecosystem" of partners, which could include "maybe even governmental" support in the form of loan guarantees or a "backstop." Her reasoning: The sheer scale of the investment and the "uncertain lifespan" of the hardware make it an exceptionally risky bet for private markets alone. 2. The Firestorm: The "Socialized Risk" Backlash The reaction was immediate and fierce. The proposal was widely labeled a "pre-emptive bailout" and "corporate welfare." Critics argued this would be a classic case of "socialized risk, privatized profit." If the $1T+ gamble on AGI failed, U.S. taxpayers would be on the hook. If it succeeded, OpenAI and its private investors would capture the astronomical profits. For a private company valued in the hundreds of billions, asking for a public safety net was seen by many as a step too far. 3. The Walk-Back: The Clarification Following the intense criticism, Friar took to LinkedIn to retract the statement. She clarified: "OpenAI is not seeking a government backstop for our infrastructure commitments. I used the word 'backstop,' and it muddied the point." Instead, she reframed her comment as a broader point: "American strength in technology will come from building real industrial capacity, which requires the private sector and government playing their part." Why This Matters (Even Though It Was Retracted) The trial balloon may have popped, but it let the air out of a very important room. This incident has forced a critical, trillion-dollar question into the open: Is the development of AGI a private, competitive venture, or is it a piece of critical national infrastructure, like the electrical grid or the interstate highway system? OpenAI's (brief) suggestion that it's the latter, a project so vast and vital it requires a public-private partnership, has fundamentally changed the conversation about who should pay for our AI future, and who should profit from it. What's your take? Was this a simple misstatement, or a glimpse into the inevitable financial reality of building AGI?

Last night I had one of those conversations that sticks with you. A small dinner, a mix of entrepreneurs and investors, talking about AI, young people, and what happens next. There was a lot of fear in the room — about automation, about jobs, about what happens if OpenAI keeps moving up the value chain and starts swallowing every vertical. Some people hoped the bubble might pop soon. Some even argued that AGI isn’t real and never will be. I don’t agree. To me, AGI isn’t the bubble — it’s the thing that bursts all the bubbles. Startups today are valued on the idea that they can replace one sliver of human work — a marketing task here, a recruitment process there. But AGI doesn’t replace software. It replaces salaries. It doesn’t want your $50 subscription. It’s coming for the $2,000–$20,000 a month we pay for human reasoning, empathy, and decision-making. If AGI captures even a slice of that, you get trillion-dollar companies — not from hype, but from re-pricing intelligence itself. So when people say, “maybe the bubble will burst,” I can’t help thinking: Yes, it will — just not the way they expect. Because the next burst won’t destroy value; it will redistribute it. And the real question isn’t whether AGI is real. It’s whether we’re ready for what happens when it stops being theoretical — and starts becoming the new baseline for human potential.

Here’s the boring truth about AGI: 1) AGI is still just science fiction + market hype. While it remains science fiction, anyone can say anything they like about it, projecting their own hopes and fears onto it – or whatever they think will get the most attention. While investors (who are not experts in AI) continue to be swayed by the hype – money will continue to flow into hype bubbles making it all look much more legitimate than it really is. 2) We will achieve AGI when enough people with enough power believe we have achieved AGI. Not when we actually achieve AGI. As it’s not well-defined, there will always be disagreement. 3) The technology will be owned by a corporation and licenced to users. There will be an intrinsic power imbalance between those with all the technology, compute and data, and those who just have a screen. There will be information disparity between the people who built it and understand it, and its users. The corporation’s marketing department will bridge the gap with anthropomorphisms that help users to see what they think their users want to see rather than what’s actually there. 4) For expert users to trust it, it will have to be really good at referencing relevant information from trusted sources written by humans. 5) To support its user in figuring things out in an empowered way – it’s going to need to ask questions that get the user to do the thinking for themselves. But it will give sound advice on how much personal data the user has shared and what the risks are of having shared that data. Including, how it may be able to use that data to manipulate the user or democracy if its owners decide to change the safety controls. 6) It will advise the user on how to reduce dependency on addictive technologies, including itself, to help the user in achieving their goals. 7) To support its user in solving the world’s biggest challenges, it will offer advice on how to get people from all different backgrounds to talk to each other face to face, instead of looking at screens. 8) It will give sound advice on whether the monthly subscription the user is paying is worthwhile, and whether they might be better served by an alternative AGI. 9) It will give the user sound advice on how to go about suing the corporation it's licenced from in the event that it gives that user bad advice that causes harm. 10) Human to human relationships will continue to be far more fulfilling than anything AGI can ever offer us – so long as we take the time to listen to ourselves and each other. Am I missing anything? Feel free to suggest anything that’s on your wish list. Remember – it’s all still just make-believe.

Another reminder that not every popular tech term makes a strong trademark. The TTAB held that CHATGPT is merely descriptive for "chatbot software for simulating conversations" because the term directly describes OpenAI's product: CHAT is a recognizable word to consumers, and GPT is an initialism (generative pre-trained transformer) that relevant consumers would also recognize as describing the product (because it appears in dictionaries and because many OpenAI's consumers are sophisticated). Remember, the more your brand reads like a product category, the harder it becomes to claim exclusivity. In the AI space especially, companies are rushing to adopt names built around terms like “AI,” “GPT,” “copilot,” and “assistant.” Those terms may help explain the technology, but they rarely make strong trademarks.

84% of people still haven't used AI directly. In 2003, only ~12% of the world had ever been online. Internet needed ~15 years to reach its first billion users. ChatGPT hit 400M weekly active users in under 2 years. Why? Internet required new physical infrastructure -> cables across oceans, telecom licenses, hardware in every home. AI rides on infrastructure that already exists. Your phone and a browser tab. The "early innings" numbers tell the real story: - India alone: 100M weekly ChatGPT users - only ~0.3% of users pay for premium AI - only ~0.04% use AI coding tools That's not "saturation." That's pre-PMF for most of the planet. The "AI bubble" crowd is repeating 2001. Dot-com valuations crashed. Internet adoption didn't. The curve kept climbing. Same pattern now. AI is significantly reshaping data center construction and design by driving the need for higher rack densities and greater power capacities to support AI workloads, especially large language models and generative AI applications .This shift is motivated by the demand for centralized, high-power-capacity facilities tailored for AI development, which include support for high-density clusters and advanced infrastructure to handle intensive computational requirements . How can we make AI more energy-efficient and sustainable? Join us for the ELLIS UnConference Workshop on Energy-Efficient AI: Models, Algorithms, and Hardware for Sustainable Intelligence taking place in Copenhagen 🇩🇰 on December 2, 2025, co-located with#EurIPS. As artificial intelligence systems grow in scale and capability, their energy demands are increasing at an unsustainable pace. This workshop addresses the urgent need for energy-efficient AI, a field that seeks to develop environmentally responsible and computationally efficient approaches to machine learning. The goal is to explore innovations that reduce energy consumption across the AI stack — from models and learning algorithms to hardware implementations — while maintaining or even enhancing performance. One source of inspiration for reaching this goal is the brain, which manages to provide intelligence with 20W. This workshop aims to bring together researchers and practitioners from diverse backgrounds to foster collaboration and cross-pollination of ideas in making AI more sustainable without compromising its transformative potential. Keynote Speakers: 🔹 Angeliki Pantazi(IBM Zurich) 🔹 Emre Neftci(Forschungszentrum Jülich) 🔹 Wilfred van der Wiel(University of Twente) 🔹 Shih-Chii Liu (ETH and University of Zurich) 🔹 Iason Chalas(ETH and IBM Zurich) 🔹 Nasir Ahmad(Radboud University Nijmegen) 🔹 Yukun Yang(Graz University of Technology) Organisers: 🔸 Wolfgang Maass (Graz University of Technology, Director of ELLIS Unit Graz) 🔸 Marcel van Gerven(Radboud University Nijmegen, Director of ELLIS unit Nijmegen) 📣 Call for contributions now open! 📅 Submission deadline: October 23, 2025 (AoE) (extended) 🔗 All info at eurips.cc/ellis

# Rohit Mittal’s Post
Rohit Mittal
1y
Sam Altman just dropped the biggest hint about AI's future. And almost everyone missed it. In a candid Reddit, Inc. AMA, he revealed OpenAI's most dramatic strategic shift. Here's what's really happening: For years, OpenAI has been the fortress of AI development. Closed source. Protected IP. Controlled access. They set the standard that every other lab followed. But everything just changed overnight: Sam didn't just hint at change. He admitted OpenAI has been "on the wrong side of history" with their closed approach. And he's not stopping there: • Planning significant Plus tier price cuts • Discussing new open source strategies • Praising competitor achievements openly The AI landscape is accelerating faster than anyone predicted: • DeepSeek matching o1 capabilities • Fast takeoff scenarios becoming more likely • o3 launching in "months not years" But here's what everyone's missing: This isn't about OpenAI losing their edge. It's about a fundamental shift in how AI development will happen: The focus is moving from: • Maintaining market dominance • Protecting intellectual property • Controlling access To: • Accelerating scientific discovery • Improving quality of life • Enabling broader innovation Think about what this means: When the leader in AI admits they were wrong about open source, how can others stay closed? When pricing trends toward accessibility, who can justify premium walls? The floodgates are about to open: Remember when tech companies hoarded their innovations? When APIs were gatekept? When algorithms were black boxes? We're at that same inflection point with AI. But this time, adoption will happen much faster: Soon, every major AI lab will need an openness strategy: • Publishing model weights • Reducing API costs • Collaborating on safety The infrastructure is ready. The models are proven. All that was missing was leadership buy-in. The market thinks this is about OpenAI's competitiveness. But that's missing the forest for the trees. This legitimizes the entire concept of open AI development. Here's what happens next: 1. More labs release model weights 2. API costs plummet across the board 3. Research becomes more collaborative 4. Innovation accelerates exponentially But there's an even more profound shift happening: The "AI arms race" narrative is dying. It's being replaced by a focus on: • Scientific advancement • Quality of life improvements • Collaborative progress The winners won't be the most secretive. They'll be the ones who help democratize AI: • Open source tools • Accessible pricing • Shared research • Safety standards The playbook is about to change: Every lab will need an openness strategy. Every company will need to justify closed systems. Every researcher will need to embrace collaboration. The internet took 20 years to transform society. Mobile took 10 years. Open AI might take 5. But Sam just accelerated everything.

Great post, Alex. Britain has an important role to play in this story. The nature of AI means that much of it will ultimately tend towards open source. Why? Because data is finite. You cannot lock it away forever, and attempts at gatekeeping will be harder for American firms to enforce as the models themselves spill into the commons. Therefore, the real pinch point isn’t the algorithms, nor even the datasets; it’s the infrastructure. Data centres. Compute. That’s where the costs spiral, and that’s where Britain must be bold. If we want to remain a serious player, we need investment in green, scalable, affordable compute at home. We’ve seen this before: the internet thrived not by being centralised in one valley, but by being distributed, borderless, and open. Britain, with its research base and tradition of public science, is well placed to lean into this European model, provided we get the foundations right.

Forget OpenAI. Europe has its own $200B playbook for winning the AI race The headlines are dominated by Silicon Valley. OpenAI. Anthropic. Google. But Europe is quietly choosing a different path. 🚨 Last week, the EU launched InvestAI, a €200B initiative to fund AI gigafactories, shared supercomputers, and public research hubs. 🚨 At the same time, Switzerland unveiled plans for a green-energy GPT-4 rival. Free, open weights, multilingual, and EU AI Act–ready. This isn’t “Europe’s OpenAI.” It’s something else entirely. It’s a bet that the future of AI should look more like CERN, and more like the internet. Because the internet didn’t succeed by being controlled by a single company. It succeeded because it was decentralised, open, and shared across borders. That same principle could define Europe’s AI strategy. ✅ Advantages of a Decentralised Model Digital Sovereignty → Europe won’t depend on U.S. tech giants. Institutions and startups can build on shared infrastructure without lock-in. Open Innovation → Open weights + public supercomputers mean more researchers, founders, and even small countries can experiment — not just those with billion-dollar budgets. Ethics First → Privacy, transparency, and sustainability built in from day one. Not bolted on later under pressure. Diversity of Perspectives → Truly multilingual models trained across 1000+ languages avoid English-only blind spots and bring global communities into the conversation. Sustainability by Design → Switzerland is powering its LLM with carbon-neutral supercomputers. Europe is baking climate goals into its AI strategy. ❌ Disadvantages Speed & Coordination → 27 countries. Countless institutions. Slower than a single lab in San Francisco. Branding & Hype → OpenAI has GPT. Anthropic has Claude. Europe risks scattering efforts across many names. Funding Discipline → Public money isn’t as ruthless as venture capital. Some projects will be political or wasteful. Talent Retention → Europe produces world-class AI talent but loses many to U.S. labs offering bigger salaries. Commercialisation Gap → OpenAI ships to a billion users overnight through Microsoft. Europe risks staying academic if industry doesn’t adopt. 🔑 The Trade-off Centralised AI (OpenAI/Anthropic): fast, clear, and commercially dominant — but closed and monopolistic. Decentralised AI (Europe): ethical, sustainable, resilient — but slower and harder to market. The internet was decentralised and that’s what made it scale globally. Europe is betting the same playbook can work for AI. The question is: will that make Europe the ethical superpower of AI or will the slower approach leave it behind? 👉 What do you think? Can a decentralised model really compete with Silicon Valley’s speed and scale? ♻️ If this resonated, share it. Someone needs this reminder today. 🔔 Follow Alex Issakova for more reflections on AI.

Why Cohere’s ex-AI research lead is betting against the scaling race Maxwell Zeff 1:52 PM PDT · October 22, 2025 AI labs are racing to build data centers as large as Manhattan, each costing billions of dollars and consuming as much energy as a small city. The effort is driven by a deep belief in “scaling” — the idea that adding more computing power to existing AI training methods will eventually yield superintelligent systems capable of performing all kinds of tasks. But a growing chorus of AI researchers say the scaling of large language models may be reaching its limits, and that other breakthroughs may be needed to improve AI performance. That’s the bet Sara Hooker, Cohere’s former VP of AI Research and a Google Brain alumna, is taking with her new startup, Adaption Labs. She co-founded the company with fellow Cohere and Google veteran @Sudip Roy, and it’s built on the idea that scaling LLMs has become an inefficient way to squeeze more performance out of AI models. Hooker, who left Cohere in August, quietly announced the startup this month to start recruiting more broadly. In an interview with TechCrunch, Hooker says Adaption Labs is building AI systems that can continuously adapt and learn from their real-world experiences, and do so extremely efficiently. She declined to share details about the methods behind this approach or whether the company relies on LLMs or another architecture. “There is a turning point now where it’s very clear that the formula of just scaling these models — scaling-pilled approaches, which are attractive but extremely boring — hasn’t produced intelligence that is able to navigate or interact with the world,” said Hooker. Adapting is the “heart of learning,” according to Hooker. For example, stub your toe when you walk past your dining room table, and you’ll learn to step more carefully around it next time. AI labs have tried to capture this idea through reinforcement learning (RL), which allows AI models to learn from their mistakes in controlled settings. However, today’s RL methods don’t help AI models in production — meaning systems already being used by customers — to learn from their mistakes in real time. They just keep stubbing their toe. Some AI labs offer consulting services to help enterprises fine-tune their AI models to their custom needs, but it comes at a price. OpenAI reportedly requires customers to spend upward of $10 million with the company to offer its consulting services on fine-tuning. https://lnkd.in/eHHkTFWS

I spent 200 hours reading AI company blogs. These 7 taught me what $120K courses couldn't. After leading AI teams for years, I've seen too many engineers get stuck in academic theory. So I dove deep into engineering blogs from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, Cohere, DeepMind, and Hugging Face to find what really matters in production. Here's what these companies understand that most don't: 🔸 OpenAI's Scaling Laws Blog https://lnkd.in/gm8UU3j5 The real insight: Model size isn't everything ↳ Compute, data, and parameters must scale together ↳ There's a sweet spot for efficiency ↳ Bigger isn't always better for your use case 🔸 Anthropic's Constitutional AI Series https://lnkd.in/giMBdmdc The real insight: Alignment isn't an afterthought ↳ Build safety into the training process ↳ Let AI critique and improve itself ↳ Values must be baked in, not bolted on 🔸 Google's Attention Is All You Need https://lnkd.in/gMGHJGAC The real insight: Simplicity beats complexity ↳ Transformers replaced complex architectures ↳ Parallel processing changed everything ↳ Sometimes removing parts makes things better 🔸 DeepMind's Chinchilla Paper https://lnkd.in/gpaSVi5A The real insight: We've been training models wrong ↳ Most models are undertrained on data ↳ Optimal ratios exist between parameters and tokens ↳ Smaller models + more data = better results 🔸 Meta's LLaMA Blog Posts https://lnkd.in/g_MFCRyi The real insight: Open source changes the game ↳ Efficiency matters more than raw performance ↳ Community innovation beats closed development ↳ Accessibility drives real-world impact 🔸 Cohere's RAG vs Fine-tuning Guide https://lnkd.in/gGb6_gEX The real insight: Choose your weapon wisely ↳ RAG for real-time, dynamic information ↳ Fine-tuning for deep domain expertise ↳ Combine both for maximum impact 🔸 Hugging Face's Model Training Insights https://lnkd.in/gX_zGg6c The real insight: Democratization drives innovation ↳ Tools matter as much as models ↳ Community knowledge compounds faster ↳ Making AI accessible creates exponential value The pattern across all these? Every breakthrough came from questioning assumptions. Think like engineers, not just researchers: • Question everything • Test at scale • Share what works • Build for impact The best part? All this knowledge is free. Most engineers just don't know where to look. # Alex Banks’ Post
[![View profile for Alex Banks]()](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/a-banks?trk=public_post_feed-actor-image)

Building a better future with AI
1d
BREAKING: Sam Altman says the AI takeoff has started.
Most people have no idea what's actually happening.
His new blog post “The Gentle Singularity” is eye-opening.
Here's what OpenAI's CEO just admitted:
1. We've crossed the point of no return
→The AI takeoff has already started
→ChatGPT is already more powerful than any human in history
→Hundreds of millions depend on it for critical tasks daily
2. Timelines are compressing
→2025: AI agents doing real cognitive work
→2026: Systems making novel scientific discoveries
→2027: Robots performing complex real-world tasks
3. Intelligence is becoming practically free
→Cost will drop to near the price of electricity
→A single ChatGPT query: 0.34 watt-hours (equivalent energy an oven uses in 1 second)
→Uses 0.000085 gallons of water (1/15th of a teaspoon)
4. The acceleration is exponential
→Scientists already 2-3x more productive with AI
→AI systems now help build better AI systems
→"A decade's worth of research in a year, or a month"
Sam says we can expect "wonders to become routine, then table stakes."
It's a recursive loop of improvement.
My takeaway:
The singularity isn't one dramatic moment where everything changes overnight.
It's already happening gradually whilst we’re climbing an exponential curve.
I believe the risk lies not in AI replacing you.
But in a small group of individuals controlling access to the most advanced AI systems in the world.
As Altmans warns, we need to “focus on making superintelligence cheap, widely available, and not too concentrated with any person, company, or country.”
When intelligence becomes a utility like electricity, who controls the grid?
That's the question we should be asking.
Follow me[Alex Banks](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/a-banks?trk=public_post-text)for daily AI highlights and insights.
